app.name: Google Veo (Flow)
app.bestFor: Best realism plus native audio and 4K. Strongest all-rounder for cinematic and narrative scenes.
app.rankBasis: Ranks at or near the top of all three cited 2026 comparisons for realism, prompt adherence and native audio, with Veo also serving as Google's flagship video model in Flow.
app.pricingFree: Limited Flow generations may be available through promotional or regional access; no dependable standing free-credit allowance
app.pricingPaid: Google AI Pro $19.99/mo — includes 1,000 monthly AI credits shared across Flow and supported Google AI tools
pros: leading realism and prompt adherence in comparisons
native dialogue, effects and ambience reduce separate audio work
Flow provides a coherent scene-building workflow
cons: credit use rises quickly for higher-quality generations
availability and model versions vary by country and plan
fine-grained timeline editing is weaker than a full video editor

app.name: Runway
app.bestFor: The creator's studio: camera moves, motion brush, reference control and an editor. Safest default.
app.rankBasis: Appears in all three cited best-video-generator lists and has the strongest established creator-tool reputation here, combining generation with an editing workspace.
app.pricingFree: Free plan with 125 one-time credits and limited model/export access
app.pricingPaid: Standard from $12/user/mo billed annually — 625 monthly credits, more models, upscale and watermark removal
pros: most complete generation-to-editing workflow
strong creative controls beyond a single prompt
frequent model and feature releases
cons: credits disappear quickly during iteration
best models and resolution options require paid tiers
output consistency still varies across longer shots

app.name: Kling AI
app.bestFor: Best value and the community workhorse for TikTok/Reels — photorealistic humans, generous free daily quota.
app.rankBasis: Included in all three cited comparisons and repeatedly highlighted for photorealistic human motion and value, making it a common community choice for short social clips.
app.pricingFree: Free plan with a daily credit allowance; exact credits and queue priority can vary by region or promotion
app.pricingPaid: Standard from about $7/mo — monthly credits, faster generation and watermark-free downloads; regional pricing varies
pros: strong human motion and photorealistic output
useful free daily access for experimentation
good value for frequent short-form creators
cons: queues can be slow on the free plan
credit rules and pricing vary by region
prompt adherence and anatomy can fail on complex motion

app.name: Pika
app.bestFor: Fast, playful short clips and effects for social. Easy on-ramp for casual creators.
app.rankBasis: Appears in both cited creator comparisons and is consistently singled out for approachable social effects rather than maximum cinematic fidelity.
app.pricingFree: Free plan with a limited monthly video-credit allowance and watermarked or restricted outputs
app.pricingPaid: Paid tiers from about $8/mo when billed annually — larger monthly credit pools, faster generation and expanded effects
pros: very easy for first-time video generators
distinctive effects work well for social posts
fast iteration on short clips
cons: short clips and effects are stronger than narrative continuity
free credits are limited
less camera and production control than Runway

app.name: Luma Dream Machine
app.bestFor: Smooth motion and image-to-video with a simple interface. Solid mid-tier option.
app.rankBasis: Selected by both cited comparisons for smooth image-to-video motion and ease of use, though it has less consistent top-rank support than Veo, Runway or Kling.
app.pricingFree: Free plan with limited monthly generations, lower priority and restricted commercial/output options
app.pricingPaid: Lite from $7.99/mo — a monthly credit pool with faster queues and broader usage rights
pros: smooth, visually appealing motion from still images
simple interface with little setup
useful keyframe and extension workflow
cons: free generations can queue slowly
credits are expensive for repeated high-resolution attempts
character and object consistency can drift

app.name: Seedance (ByteDance)
app.bestFor: Tops raw model benchmarks (Artificial Analysis) but less packaged as a consumer product.
app.rankBasis: The cited model ranking places Seedance among the raw quality leaders, but independent consumer-product reviews remain thin because access is primarily through ByteDance platforms and APIs.
app.pricingPaid: Consumption-based API pricing through supported ByteDance/Volcengine platforms; no stable public flat subscription for this model page
pros: very strong benchmarked visual quality
good multi-shot and narrative coherence
competitive motion and prompt following
cons: not packaged as a consistent global consumer app
pricing and access depend on platform and region
limited independent long-term user-review evidence

skill.name: Open-Sora (hpcaitech)
skill.bestFor: The category-defining open-source video generator (~29k GitHub★) — text/image-to-video with performance comparable to HunyuanVideo on VBench.
skill.rankBasis: About 29k GitHub stars and top placement in the cited open-source model comparison make Open-Sora the highest-adoption general open video project in this track.
pros: large research and contributor community
transparent training and inference stack
useful baseline for experimentation and fine-tuning
cons: substantial GPU requirements
setup is research-oriented rather than consumer-friendly
quality and temporal consistency trail the best closed models

skill.name: Wan (Alibaba, Wan2.2)
skill.bestFor: Alibaba's Wan (~16.8k GitHub★) — widely regarded as the strongest open-weight text-to-video model; 1.3B variant runs on 8GB VRAM.
skill.rankBasis: Roughly 16.8k GitHub stars plus strong placement in open-video roundups support Wan as one of the most adopted and capable open-weight families, with a practical small model.
pros: excellent open-weight quality for its size
small variant lowers hardware barriers
active official releases and broad community support
cons: larger checkpoints still need high-end GPUs
generation is slow without optimization
licensing and commercial terms must be checked per checkpoint

skill.name: HunyuanVideo (Tencent)
skill.bestFor: Tencent's 13B open video model (~12.4k GitHub★) — a benchmark reference for open-source cinematic text-to-video quality.
skill.rankBasis: Approximately 12.4k GitHub stars and repeated inclusion in open-source video comparisons make HunyuanVideo a benchmark reference for large cinematic open models.
pros: strong cinematic detail among open models
official Tencent research release
widely supported by community workflows
cons: very high VRAM and storage demands
slow inference on consumer hardware
complex setup compared with hosted generators

skill.name: CogVideo / CogVideoX (Zhipu)
skill.bestFor: Apache-2.0 CogVideoX (~12.9k GitHub★) — loads natively in HF Diffusers, strong prompt following and smooth frames; a go-to for developers.
skill.rankBasis: About 12.9k GitHub stars, Apache-2.0 licensing and first-class Diffusers support give CogVideoX unusually strong developer adoption in this cohort.
pros: permissive license and mature developer tooling
good prompt following for an open model
easier integration through Hugging Face
cons: large models still require significant VRAM
short clips and limited native editing controls
visual quality varies with quantization and hardware settings

skill.name: LTX-Video (Lightricks)
skill.bestFor: Lightricks' real-time DiT video model (~10.7k GitHub★) — notable for fast, production-capable generation and active releases.
skill.rankBasis: Roughly 10.7k GitHub stars and recognition in the cited open-model comparison distinguish LTX-Video for unusually fast generation and active production-oriented releases.
pros: much faster inference than many large open models
active releases and ecosystem integrations
practical for iterative production workflows
cons: best performance still needs modern GPUs
fast modes trade some detail and consistency
model/version choices can be confusing

skill.name: Mochi 1 (Genmo)
skill.bestFor: Genmo's Apache-2.0 Mochi 1 (~3.7k GitHub★) — a recognized open text-to-video model, lower adoption than the leaders above.
skill.rankBasis: About 3.7k GitHub stars and an Apache-2.0 release make Mochi a credible open model, but adoption is materially below the five projects ranked above it.
features: Open model weights and inference code
pros: permissive open-source release
good motion for a first-generation open model
clear research implementation
cons: very demanding hardware footprint
slower project momentum than newer rivals
limited editing and conditioning features

skill.name: Claude Code Video Toolkit (digitalsamba)
skill.bestFor: The standout Agent Skill for the space (~1.8k GitHub★): a skills bundle (Remotion, FFmpeg, voiceover, music) that scripts and renders an MP4 from one prompt.
skill.rankBasis: Around 1.8k GitHub stars plus coverage in the cited Claude Code ecosystem make this the best-adopted agent skill here, well ahead of the two thinner workflow skills below.
pros: covers scripting, assets, audio and rendering end to end
produces editable code rather than only a generated clip
well documented for agent-driven workflows
cons: requires Node, FFmpeg and external media services
render quality depends on generated code and assets
not a foundation video model

skill.name: video-generation (ByteDance DeerFlow)
skill.bestFor: Structured-JSON prompt → video skill from ByteDance's DeerFlow SuperAgent, with reference-image / first-last-frame guidance.
skill.rankBasis: Backed by ByteDance's cited DeerFlow repository and a concrete public SKILL.md, but the individual skill has no independent star count or meaningful review corpus.
skill.pricingFree: Free & open-source skill; underlying video model/API costs may apply
pros: official example inside a larger agent framework
clear structured parameters for repeatable calls
supports reference and boundary-frame workflows
cons: depends on configured external generation providers
little independent feedback on the skill itself
not useful as a standalone video engine

skill.name: claude-remotion-skill (haidrrrry)
skill.bestFor: Open-source skill teaching Claude to create/edit motion-graphics videos with Remotion — B-roll, captions, sound design from a prompt. Thin/single-author.
skill.rankBasis: A genuine public Remotion skill, but it is a thin single-author project with limited stars and no substantial independent review signal.
pros: inspectable code-first video workflow
useful conventions for captions and motion graphics
easy to customize for a specific brand
cons: very limited adoption evidence
requires Remotion and media-development knowledge
does not generate source footage by itself

notes: Rebuilt to rank by real reputation/adoption, not form factor (kind is a metadata tag only). The open-source video-generation models are the category leaders and correctly outrank the agent skills: Open-Sora (~29k★) is the recognized open leader, followed by Wan, HunyuanVideo, CogVideoX and LTX-Video. Agent Skills (Video Toolkit, DeerFlow, Remotion) orchestrate a render pipeline and sit below the models by adoption. Previous aggregator MCP entries (fal, Replicate, mcp.film) and the thin inference.sh listing were removed; Sora stays omitted (OpenAI shut the Sora app 2026-04-26).
